explanation: The natural language statement specifies a condition and a required state that must hold whenever that condition is true. "When the ventilator is in Start-up or VentilationOff mode" is translated to the disjunction of the atomic propositions 'StartUpMode' and 'StandbyMode' (since StandbyMode is defined as the same as VentilationOff Mode). "the in valve pressure shall be set to close and the out valve shall be open" is translated to the conjunction of 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen'. The phrase "When...shall be" implies a global implication. Thus, the LTL property states that globally (G), if the system is in StartUpMode or StandbyMode, then it must be true that the in valve is closed and the out valve is open.

The property states that globally (G), whenever the system is in 'Start-up Mode' (StartUpMode) or 'VentilationOff Mode' (StandbyMode, as indicated in its description), it must always be the case that the 'inspiratory valve is close' (inValveClose) AND 'the out valve is open' (outValveOpen). This directly translates the conditional requirement 'When A, then B' into a global implication in LTL.

The natural language statement specifies a condition and a requirement that must hold whenever that condition is true. The condition is that the ventilator is in 'Start-up mode' or 'VentilationOff mode'. 'Start-up mode' directly maps to the atomic proposition 'StartUpMode'. 'VentilationOff mode' is explicitly stated in the description of 'StandbyMode' as being the same, so 'StandbyMode' is used. These two conditions are connected by an 'or'. The requirement is that 'the in valve pressure shall be set to close' (mapped to 'inValveClose') AND 'the out valve shall be open' (mapped to 'outValveOpen'). These two requirements are connected by an 'and'. The 'When...shall be' phrasing indicates a global implication: whenever the condition (Start-up or VentilationOff mode) is true, the consequence (in valve closed and out valve open) must also be true at that instant. This is best captured by a global (G) implication, where the antecedent is the disjunction of the modes and the consequent is the conjunction of the valve states.

The property states that globally, whenever the ventilator is in 'Start-up Mode' or 'Standby Mode' (which is synonymous with 'VentilationOff Mode'), it must always be the case that the 'in valve' is closed and the 'out valve' is open. This directly captures the 'When X, Y shall be Z' structure of the input sentence, using a global implication.

The natural language statement specifies a global requirement: whenever the ventilator is in 'Start-up' mode or 'VentilationOff' mode (which is equivalent to Standby Mode), two conditions must hold simultaneously: the inspiratory valve must be closed, and the expiratory valve must be open. The LTL formula captures this by using the 'G' (Globally) operator to assert that the implication always holds. The antecedent of the implication is the disjunction of 'StartUpMode' and 'StandbyMode', representing 'Start-up or VentilationOff mode'. The consequent is the conjunction of 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen', representing 'the in valve pressure shall be set to close and the out valve shall be open'.

The natural language statement requires that whenever the ventilator is in 'Start-up' or 'VentilationOff' mode, two specific conditions must simultaneously be true: the 'in valve' must be closed, and the 'out valve' must be open. The 'Start-up' mode is directly represented by 'StartUpMode'. The 'VentilationOff' mode is represented by 'StandbyMode', as per its description. The conjunction (OR) of these two modes forms the precondition. The consequences 'in valve close' and 'out valve open' are represented by 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' respectively, which must both be true, hence an 'AND' is used. The 'When X, Y shall be Z' structure translates to a global implication: 'Globally (if (StartUpMode OR StandbyMode) then (inValveClose AND outValveOpen))'. The 'G' operator ensures this property holds at all times.

The natural language statement specifies a condition under which a certain state must hold. 'When the ventilator is in Start-up or VentilationOff mode' translates to the disjunction of the atomic propositions 'StartUpMode' and 'StandbyMode' (since StandbyMode is explicitly described as the same as VentilationOff Mode). 'the in valve pressure shall be set to close and the out valve shall be open' translates to the conjunction of 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen'. The phrase 'When ... shall be' indicates that this condition-consequence relationship must hold globally, at all times. Therefore, the LTL property is a global implication: G((StartUpMode | StandbyMode) -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)).

The property states that globally, whenever the ventilator is in 'Start-up Mode' or 'Standby Mode' (which is also referred to as 'VentilationOff Mode'), it must always be the case that the inspiratory valve is closed and the out valve is open. This captures the requirement that the specified valve states are maintained as long as the system is in either of these operational modes.

The LTL property states that globally (G), whenever the system is in 'Start-up Mode' (StartUpMode) or 'VentilationOff Mode' (StandbyMode), it must be the case that the 'in valve' is closed (inValveClose) AND the 'out valve' is open (outValveOpen). This captures the requirement that these valve states are maintained as long as the system is in either of those specified modes.

The LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the ventilator is in 'Start-up mode' or 'VentilationOff mode' (which is synonymous with 'Standby Mode'), the inspiratory valve must be closed, and the expiratory valve must be open. The disjunction `StartUpMode | StandbyMode` correctly represents 'Start-up or VentilationOff mode'. The conjunction `inValveClose & outValveOpen` represents 'the in valve pressure shall be set to close and the out valve shall be open'. The global operator `G` combined with the implication `->` ensures that this condition-consequence relationship holds at all times.
